replies.author: @bad-people6510
replies.text: I've said it before, and I'll say it again. Westerns, with their far greater story telling limitations, dominated the film industry for 75 years. That's an entire lifetime. Ultimately they didn't go away because people stopped wanting to watch them, they went away because film makers stopped wanting to make them. And even after they dropped off, they are still capable of radical success when they do pop up. Some of the best westerns ever made were from the 80s and 90s. Any genre can succeed at any time, it just has to be good and not come from a poisoned reputation.

replies.author: @Mazza5910
replies.text:
I think Disney+ had some big issues they didn’t really know how to address:
1) very late to the media subscription model where competitors like Netflix had a wide range of shows on there which wasn’t something Disney could provide, the movies they had were mainly nostalgia pieces that we all watched to remember our childhoods but isn’t something we could watch day in and day out.
2) “woke ideologies infiltrating the writing of shows and scripts”
3) not understanding their consumer base enough to accurately depict well loved characters or create new ones to pull viewers in.
4) bad casting: where they used big actors as the key influencers to pull people in not considering the fact the movies actually need to be good and that not every actor big actor is best for the role, but also being unable to bring new great actors in
5) time: because they had to play catch up they thought they could solve all of these issues with money, however all the shows felt rushed, unoriginal and just generally bad

replies.text:
Unironically? Disney itself has told it's entire audience to stay home. By promising that in just a few weeks, you can watch all these movies in your living room.

Why on earth would anyone go to the movie theater now, spend 100 bucks for their family to see the newest ok movie, when for a few bucks a month, they can see it in their living room, without annoying teens, crying babies, and chatty folk ruining it in a theater? It's not the movies. It's the movie experience itself that has lost its value proposition. Bar the odd experience, Disney shoots its own box office in the back with Disney+.

My family loved going to the movies. But now, if there is any film I'm just "sorta" interested in, or heard good things about, I wait. And so does half the casual audience that used to make these things profitable

author: @Badmunky64
text:
It's all about momentum.  During the height of MCU momentum, mediocre movies like Ant-Man 2 and Capt Marvel were still making a billion and even during covid, okay-ish films like 10 Rings did well enough.  But once you have a trend of mediocre or crap films for 5 years, that momentum is dead, and the train has stopped moving.   

There has been so much brand damage that, you need 2 or 3 amazing films back-to-back in order to start generating buzz to get it moving again.  Something I have zero faith in Disney doing at this point.

replies.author: @GideonRavenor712
replies.text:
That is still partially a problem on the studios' end.

Disney especially is renowned for demanding a bigger cut out of ticket sales to even have their films be aired in a theater (a card they've played because they have been confident many of their films will be top-sellers), and the overall inflation towards everything hasn't helped that either.

The studios also ALL attempted to cut out both theaters and physical sales during the pandemic because somehow they thought that state of affairs would be eternal, AND that people would just be down to have an endless number of SINGULAR streaming services they were paying for.

Instead, nope, not a single studio has made money off of their streaming service, all have lost. Sony is so far the only company that quit such a project first. So now they have the worst of all outcomes: they snubbed theater and physical sales to where it's all been diminished and won't revitalize without serious effort, AND their big gamble to try and centralize the markets around themselves have just led to massive money sinks for these companies.

That, and the movie needs to justify the 'theater experience' now because of that price. 

So to me it comes down to a few things: Hollywood at large needs to up their quality, they need to tighten their budgets to be more reasonable with the overall economy, and they need to cut theaters slack if they want to sustain them.

But I don't think any of them are likely to drastically change course without taking serious heat financially. They're more ready to blame Trump than take responsibility for their own choices.

I don't think Fox even learned a lesson despite relenting to his demand. Pretty sure the future Deadpool films are only rated R because he proved it worked, but they still see the genre as "for kids" regardless.

replies.author: @SynsityGW
replies.text: yeah idk it's pretty simple, too. dudes like superhero movies because our stupid monkey brains like to imagine ourselves as heroes for biological reasons and the films and characters inspire us. when you take all these heroes and say "fuck men, all the male ones are now weak and pathetic and take a back seat, if you want to be inspired perhaps you can try our far more powerful and way more snarky and sarcastic female replacement! don't worry the male characters you loved are still here, to be berated and insulted and blamed constantly throughout the films", no matter what a bunch of soy social media "men" say - it simply loses the appeal. like for endgame they could get away with some of it because we were already invested in characters we found inspirational and likable. but now having to introduce new ones? and they *still* continue to make them laughing stocks who seemingly only exist to have a lesser man to compare their far greater female counterparts to. who is this trying to appeal to? who are the people in the marketing department who convinced a bunch of billionaires that women love to fantasize about being heroic and love brave, self sacrificing, inspiring characters? they don't. obviously it's a generalization, but women prefer things like reality television and romance fiction with lots of interpersonal drama and the characters they fantasize about being are the ones like bella in twilight who are special and are the target of everyone's lust, because they have monkey brains like us, just different. action sequences put them to sleep and inspiring characters only matter if they're cool enough to make them tingle, which the female ones do not. and they haven't been subtle about it, nothing i'm saying can even be countered because they've admitted it 100 times over whether by slogans like "the force is female", popular characters being replaced by superior female counterparts like thor and loki and iron man and they were about to do it to dr. strange, or by just rebooting a franchise with a strong girlboss at the helm like galadriel in the rings of power and rey in star wars and a billion other examples. who are these films made for? they've said repeatedly that it's *super* important to see yourself represented in media. so obviously they were made for women. who the fuck talked people into flushing literally billions of dollars down the drain taking every male franchise and hobby and not even saying "let's bring in the female demographic" but saying "let's kick the men out and replace them with women". how could anyone with a brain believe that would work? i think sadly the answer is, it's not possible. this had to have been done knowingly. just a bunch of bitter people, willing to sacrifice money, jobs and beloved IPs all in the name of sticking it to half the world because of their own unresolved trauma most likely. it's pretty pathetic that it was allowed to occur and now, it's done. the trust is gone. fantastic four was good? i read that it was going to be all about how susan storm wasn't just eye candy, she was awesome and just as smart as reed richards and that johnny was no longer a toxic womanizer but a modern soy man. so i just didn't see it. and i don't really intend to, despite being told that it was good by people i generally trust. i've moved onto other hobbies and IPs. they need to send out messaging that basically says "we are back to trying to appeal these male dominated franchises to the men who once loved them" but they would be raked over the coals by the rest of hollywood, so it's just over. i'm blathering a little but i do think this is how it all happened and why it can't be fixed at this point. even the "i don't care about your culture wars, go away" type of guy has been pushed out because his monkey brain is no longer being inspired so once the magic of the old characters wore off, they just stopped caring. add to that the fact that their new female replacements dress like fucking quakers and natalie portman has cgi biceps and it's difficult to see how they would appeal to a male demographic in any way shape or form. the experiment to see if you could overwrite our biological programming with nonstop social messaging, peer pressure, fear of being ostracized and gaslighting is over, it failed.

author: @MarQDog
text: I agree big time with the point about Pedro Pascal. but as for the others, I think the best way to explain the “slump” is that people are tired of every movie clearly being the setup of a multi-character, multi-storyline franchise. Focus on ONE character, and tell ONE story from start to finish, in ONE movie. Make a solid foundation before you build a skyscraper. Iron Man 1 was this way. Matrix 1. As a kid, I remember loving comics and animations that opened and shut a story in one episode. And while cliffhangers were exciting, I’d actually cringe a bit at comics with “Episode 1 of 15” stamped across the headline. Sometimes you just want a day in the life, not a life in the life. Give me one good story and let me decide if I want more

replies.author: @itisabird
replies.text:
@daoneils3783 I'm not the one complaining about the US, but still think that you should have your arguments straight. Marvel and DC are US companies (please remember that America is the name of the whole continent), but they wouldn't have created superheroes without the inspiration from European mythology, such as Thor, Hercules and more. Movies where invented through a collaborative process between many scientists who added small steps to it, from the early British Eadweard Muybridge until the French Lumiere brothers invented what we now call cinemas. Regarding smartphones, before the original Apple iPhone the high-end European Nokia phones offered about the same features. Apple gave them a better interface with a proper touch screen, but everything was based on the foundations that already existed. And, regarding the invention of Internet, there were many projects in Europe similar to its US origin, Arpanet. Some of them were the French CYCLADES, the Spanish Iberpac or the British NPL Network. 

With this, I don't want to undervalue the contributions of US to the tech sector, but I've become under the impression that many people believe that the world would be living in caves without them, which is obviously wrong. The world advanced from the Stone Age to the current times without depending on any single country that still exists, and it will still advance even after any of the countries that exist today disappears.

replies.author: @dontcare7086
replies.text: I totally agree with this. It used to be a movie didn't become available to rent for a year or year and half from the date of release. For the price of a single adult movie ticket you can rent it from home as you said 30 days after release. They also shoot themselves in the foot by having it free on something like hbo. I wait 30 days, get a prepaid debit card from any store usually CVS, create a new prime account with it and load the exact amount a month of prime costs. Then you can get a 7 day free trial of hbo, cinemax, and 50 other services. I watched Superman with that free trial, got caught up with a few apple shows I like, got the free amc trial, and finished the month with some Starz movies and cinemax movies with those free trials. You can do that every single month I've done it for years now.

replies.text: ​​​@komori29for me in Ireland when I went to see F1 it cost me what would be 21 dollars for the ticket alone. I dont know what you guys pay over in the US but this has gotten insane here.. its a multi-factor issue that's causing this, its equally because studios are pulling the movies from cinemas earlier to go to streaming, which kills cinemas profits as the longer they stay in cinemas the more % the cinemas get if i recall correctly? So both the cinema chains and studios are fighting each other over the profits. Studios should realise the importance of physical media and how much money they truly gained from that market..yes I know streaming is here to stay but if they pushed the bluray/dvd market for added bonuses and features etc they could recoup more and let the movies have prolonged cinematic release runs and not ridiculously short turnovers within a month of release date to streaming online debut..

replies.author: @SkorjOlafsen
replies.text:
@kimmccarthy7747 I just re-watched the SW OT (the theatrical cut), and you know what, it aged very well indeed.  CGI always ages like shit, but practical effects stay good.  It doesn't matter that it's obviously a puppet, it's endearing and the mind just goes with it.  The kids loved it, didn't mind the effects at all, just like the old-school Doctor Who.    

Yeah, you're going to need CGI effects to do the FF, because of their particular powers (at least the stretching and the flames), but the movie made surprising little use of those powers.  It was effects-heavy because of the villains and plot they chose, much like Superman.  No one is ever going to be "wowed" again by obvious effects, guys, just give it up already.

replies.author: @-newuser-707
replies.text:
​@marthflores3515 I gave the new Superman 7 out of 10, and FF 7.1. I'm revising the Superman score down to 5/10. Once you realize what's really being pushed, and how naively insulting it is to populations, it's a particularly reckless and also shamelessly deceptive movie. Pay very close attention to some of the casting choices. 

FF is boring for two of its three acts, having a twisted false premise designed to appeal to a particular demographic, and an illogical unrealistic fantasy outcome, albeit done well. For its clarity alone, it's better than Gunn's Superman. It's from the POV of those who made it, exposing their genuine overall fears. I revise its score down to 6 out of 10.

It’s $15,000-$75,000 per shot on big projects. 

Movies in general have become inflated to make this isn’t just from CGI and setting, this is also for equipment, salaries, teams, effects, stunts, props, renting props, costumes, make up, film, editing, revising, and more unforeseen expenses. This isn’t just for movies but video games and media in general has become inflated

Disney has screwed themselves.

Adult fans are lost to them as they move on to the next stages of life. After a certain age, people don’t have the desire and motivation to keep their obsessions over movies and shows of their youth. 

Kids are lost to them because they never had their love and attention in the first place. They are either apathetic or even despise their awful, politically-driven products.

Their only option left is to create big spectacle movies and promote the hell out of them, which is not sustainable because it costs too much and they suck.

replies.author: @Deletirium
replies.text:
No, superhero fatigue is absolutely a problem, that just happened to be exacerbated and sped up by shitty moviemaking. I've never liked superheros - it's an incredibly predictable, low-stakes genre. Invincible people who always pull the plot out of the fire at the 11th hour, origin stories, characters who never truly die... the only reason audiences put up with it for so long was that the underlying movies were surprisingly good, even apart from being about superheroes. 

There will always be a specific following that like anything superhero, and that's fine, but most non-comic book general movie going audiences are not. 

Even if the movies were still good (and they haven't been since Endgame), people would still be yawning at the formulaic plots and market oversaturation by now. It's gotten to a point where any film, regardless of genre, when it mentions a potential "expanded cinematic universe" people instantly roll their eyes and drop it there.

replies.text:
It's easy to say that in hindsight. Had they gone with that concept, there would've been plenty of fans, critics and general audiences alike discussing why Disney didn't strike when the iron was still hot. Of course there was the Pandemic that the MCU would've completely bypassed, but nobody could foresee that. 

Plus, movies like 'Furiosa', 'Kingdom of the Planat of the Apes', and the last two Mission Impossible films took longish waits between sequels from pre-2020 world. And while not all were box office failures, they didn't exactly set the BO on fire either, in accordance with each of their budgets anyway. So the theory that Disney should've waited doesn't really hold up that well.

replies.author: @billjacobs521
replies.text: Nah, that would have killed the hype. But they should have shrunk down the scope. We needed to back down to something like another Ant-Man movie, a smaller-scale story with smaller stakes, especially since they wanted to introduce new superheroes. Ms Marvel was dumb but at least the right idea. Especially after the snap; we could have had so many good stories about that. Instead, they tried to just keep it at the fever-pitch. It's like trying to keep a big action scene going for 40 solid minutes without a break; it's exhausting and people check out. You have to release some of the tension, vary things up, we can't just have a calvcade of universe-ending threats like Gor, the Scarlet Witch, and Kang, all back to back.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
Experiencing complications and dangers and growing to achieve some kind of resolution is most stories. But sure, you can vary the scale or nature of the danger and/or the scale or nature of the resolution. And not every story involved bucketloads of spandex.

Escalation can be a trap. A lot of tv shows had the problem of starting with an idiosyncratic plot and human drama. Then by series 2 and 3, they escalate to make it about the world being at risk. It's often a cheap way to try to increase interest. But if they audience needs the world to be at threat, then it's harder to go back down to something smaller scale - smaller scales of problem, personal stakes etc. And smaller stakes might require more skill. If smaller stakes don't justify a longer term property, it's possible to tell an affecting story, make a profit, and shut up shop before people are sick of you. The Godfather film series only tried for 3 films. They didn't shake the audience down for another 10.

As for 'it all works out in the end', they did kill Tony Stark. But their character deaths were calculated. He got a story arc of growth and sacrifice, but writing out Steve Rogers and RDJ seemed to just be clearing the decks for younger actors. They killed off a couple minor characters and one somewhat significant character (but that was after people started to tune out). If character death is more corporate pragmatism, it eliminates one source of tension and a feeling of uncertainty.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
You've just described the genre... and there's nothing wrong with it per se.  Every genre has certain beats it has to hit or else it isn't that genre.  The problem has been massive oversaturation.  There are only so many times most folk can sit down and watch the same plots and formats.

A lot of the pre-Endgame MCU movies weren't really that great, but they were OK and building towards a finale so people stuck with it.  Once that finale was over they needed to let the dust settle and rest the franchise.  It was obvious interest would wane, but the money men couldn't see it.  They thought they could keep churning out more and more and keep raking in the money.  Instead they've killed their golden goose.

@jjc5407 They don't want to leave if there's still money on the table. if they think the product has long-term life, then they need to manage it for sustainability. But it tastes turned, then they didn't cash in as hard as they could. If they think anything is just a fad, then sell as hard as possible. They're milking their prized cash cow to death.

Disney bought Star Wars, Marvel (probably because their in-house IP wasn't sufficiently impressive). They churned out content as hard as possible. But unless appetite was infinite, there was always the likelihood of arriving at a tomorrow. They couldn't ride Star Wars and Marvel for 100 years. Maybe they thought they could force people to like these properties for millennia. Otherwise, there was always going to be a tomorrow and a conversation of 'ok, what do we do next?'.

​@justforever96 superman dealing with a rash of purse snatchings could be quite the interesting movie.

A thing I've thought about superman for a while is that he is actually the ideal character for lower stake issues rather than city destroying events.

Imagine a movie about superman trying to stop some gangs. he can easily stop them and send them to jail, but what if they have friends in high places?
I feel lex luthor is wasted being always the direct oposition of superman. He should be like a masterminde behind scenes, not heading directly to face superman or building a whatever murder clone bot 3000 to kill him, but simply being corrupt, aiding crime and damaging people for profit without nothing sticking to him. You could have clark trying to perform actual journalism to expose luthors corrupt schemes, and even that failing because luthor is an important share holder of The Planet and the editor decides to shelve the story.

I know its hard, but when your superhero is invincible, you need to be able to hurt him beyond just "punching him harder"

replies.author: @notgonnapay
replies.text:
@cecilthecavetroll9743 That’s not even close to the truth. The VFX team was comprised of 35 people who were paid 1/4 to 1/3 of the film’s $15 million budget and reused a bunch of pre-existing assets. They also built a kitchen and hired a sushi chef for them for when they inevitably had to work long hours. The VFX artists were quite literally treated better than any team that’s worked for Hollywood in the recent past.
A coherent vision and the director’s ability to work directly with the VFX team are what made the effects so good.
A quote from the director, Kiyoko Shibuya: 
“We had a very young compositor—that was his role inside the company—but it turned out he loved VFX. He did some water simulations at home and brought them to the office one day, We said, ‘Oh wow, this is some pretty high-quality water simulation!’ That allowed me to write in more scenes that took place on the ocean. But it didn’t fundamentally change the story, just the ratio of shots we took. It also helped that I was the writer, director and VFX supervisor. That meant there were a lot of efficiencies happening within my head, like knowing the ultimate shot and how it would look on-screen, so at evey stage of production, we knew that we were inching ourselves closer to that final product.”

replies.author: @mr.sinjin-smyth
replies.text: Westerns first became popular in the 1930s, with Stagecoach (1939) putting it on the mainstream, and would remain super popular until the 1970s, when gritty action films and the arrival of Star Wars placed it on the backseat. By the 1980s, the genre was quite dead, though enjoyed some occasional sparks of magic in the years since like Pale Rider (1985), Silverado (1985), Young Guns (1988), Dances With Wolves (1990), Back To The Future 3 (1990), Unforgiven (1992), Tombstone (1993), and Open Range (2003).

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
As Drinker said "superhero fatigue" is absolutely a thing.  The market has been seriously oversaturated.  Most of these movies are about characters even comic book nerds would struggle to name.  The few films that succeed are those that provide some novelty.  3 Spider-Men and all the villains from the first two's movies?  Surefire massive hit.  The Eternals?  Who cares?

In the last 17 years since Iron Man there have been over 70 Marvel, DC, Sony and other live action superhero movies and thousands of hours of superhero live action TV shows, not to mention numerous animations.  Enough already.  The general audience has checked out.

There are only so many characters they can and will invest in, only so many variations in the same basic plots, only so many times you can have huge battles that destroy half a city.  The genre has become boring.  Audiences want new, they want different, they want innovation.  They'll watch the occasional superhero movie, but they're no longer going to turn up in their droves for most of them.

Producing a couple of dozen interconnected movies with an overarching story arc was something that had never been done before in film.  It was a remarkable achievement, but it should have stopped with Endgame.  At the very least the MCU and really the entire genre needed a rest.

replies.author: @Rogue-a-Pogue
replies.text:
@Thomasmemoryscentral I really liked Werewolf By Night and I think that was the sort of thing that Disney should've done more of with the Disney+ service. 
Instead of giving inexperienced writers/directors $200+ million dollar budgets and theatrical releases set them an obscure character, a modest budget and one hour run-time to tell a compelling story. Do this with a dozen different director/writer teams and not only do you generate a lot of content but you also use the competition between them to create better content, with the potential carrot of a series or theatrical movie to motivate them further. Then you see which ones the public and/or critical audience gravitates towards and then you have your next potential break-out success. At worse, shows like Ironheart, Secret Invasion and Echo could have be mitigated into a single hour instead of an entire series.

replies.author: @figodwnnieto2581
replies.text:
I view anime stuff as separate to live action stuff. By the last Avengers film and Thor Ragnarok I was starting to get bored of superhero films even with ones I liked, and while it was somewhat an MCU fatigue, it spread onto other stuff too as I became uninterested in watching other franchise releases as well. Although the other releases didn't seem to get good reviews often anyway, so no loss really. The only live action stuff I think people weren't fatigued by were the comedy heavy films like Guardians of the Galaxy and Deadpool, as you could easily enjoy them not even being a fan of superhero stuff. 

I'm not someone who is really into superhero stuff, but I did get into enjoying a lot of things with MCU when they were putting out good films in the beginning. I think superhero stuff needs something like Dark Knight to bring people back into enjoying the genre. However give it a couple of years of not having multiple superhero Hollywood films first maybe.

author: @ENTERtheCREATOR
text: Honestly, VFX can still look REALLY good at a budget of $10 million. The producers, director, and writer(s) just need to buckle down, agree on a vision, and push that vision through without the dreaded, "We'll fix it in post," or, "I think that should move differently, can we change it?" Once the vision is locked down, and the production can say with a reasonable degree of accuracy, "We have 163 VFX shots, 44 of them are in need of CGI, and 3 of them are full CGI renders," the easier and cheaper it is to produce good VFX for a film.

replies.author: @ForeverLaxx
replies.text: This is the real problem, honestly. Too many directors see CG and greenscreen as an editorial "safety net" that they defer so many things that could be fixed in that moment to "fixing it in post." Combine this wasteful mentality with heavily inflated advertising budgets and you get these crazy totals of spent money that they'll never hope of making back unless the film is grossing a billion on opening weekend.

author: @TheFiftyQuid
text: The movie business really shot themselves in the foot.  When I was younger, people lined up for movies.  It sucked, but if you wanted to see that movie opening weekend that's what you had to do.  Then, even if the movie was a complete flop, you still had the VHS release.  That first day that your local video store had the movie.  Video stores had to buy those copies.  I remember big releases being over $100 for one VHS tape.  At $3.99 a rental, that video store owner had to rent the copy 30, sometimes 40 times just to break even.  The movie industry had the big screen, they had the little screen, then they had retail.  A movie like Raiders of the Lost Ark could make a profit in theatre, then in video stores, then in retail.  Hell, it still makes money streaming today.  New movies don't get this luxury.  All of their profit needs to come all at once, mainly from the big screen.  Only a faction comes from streaming, and retail is barely hanging on.  Imagine a movie like Clerks being released today.  It wouldn't have had a chance to become a cult classic.  It would have been buried at the bottom of a list of streaming movies that week.

replies.author: @timothymelton2214
replies.text: Personally I’ve never used the term “woke” to describe anything or anyone. It’s never been a part of my vocabulary. I’ve just watched others use it and interpreted its ever changing meaning. It’s been one of the fastest evolving words I’ve ever seen in my 50 ish years of using words! Starting off as black vernacular for someone who is both actively aware of the passive racism typical in the US and seeks to counter it. Rapidly moving to a wider context of meaning all people who are allies to that struggle. Then almost overnight morphing into a catchall for someone who shows general decency towards others. Then it was almost instantly picked up by the American right as a slur for hyper performative liberalism. Where is hung out for a bit but now seems, as best I can tell from reading threads and conversations, to be little more than a vague derogatory term to throw at someone you kind of don’t like and don’t really want to deal with or confront directly or honestly. (That’s fair sometimes we don’t.) In any event it’s been a wild ride for “woke” and, personally, I’m ready for it to fade away. But you can’t control language. Oh well.
